title: The Pacific: Pearl Harbor to Tokyo Bay
summary: A war fought across a third of the planet's surface: it opened with a Sunday-morning surprise attack on Hawaii, turned on five minutes at Midway, and ended on a battleship's deck in Tokyo Bay less than four years later.

waypoints.title: A date which will live in infamy
waypoints.label: The attack
waypoints.narrative: At 7:55 on Sunday morning, 7 December 1941, 353 aircraft from six Japanese carriers struck the US Pacific Fleet at anchor. In under two hours they sank or damaged all eight battleships and killed 2,403 Americans — 1,177 of them aboard USS Arizona, which exploded and sank in nine minutes. But the fleet's aircraft carriers were at sea, the fuel farms and repair yards survived, and a divided America woke united. Japan had planned a knockout; it got, in Admiral Yamamoto's reputed words, a sleeping giant.

image.caption: USS Arizona burning after the attack, 7 December 1941 (US National Archives)

waypoints.title: Five minutes at Midway
waypoints.label: The turning point
waypoints.narrative: Six months after Pearl Harbor, US codebreakers read enough of Japan's naval cipher to know the next target: Midway. Admiral Nimitz set an ambush with three carriers — including Yorktown, patched back together in 72 hours after the Coral Sea. On the morning of 4 June 1942, American dive bombers caught the Japanese carriers refuelling and rearming; in about five minutes, Akagi, Kaga and Sōryū were burning wrecks, Hiryū following by nightfall. Japan lost four fleet carriers and its irreplaceable veteran aircrews. The Pacific initiative changed hands in a single morning and never changed back.

image.caption: USS Yorktown struck by a torpedo at Midway, 4 June 1942

waypoints.title: Guadalcanal: the first step back
waypoints.label: The offensive begins
waypoints.narrative: In August 1942 US Marines waded ashore on a malarial island few Americans could find on a map, to seize an airfield that threatened the lifeline to Australia. Six months of jungle fighting, night naval battles in 'Ironbottom Sound', and constant air combat followed — a grinding attritional campaign that neither side could afford but Japan could afford less. When the Japanese evacuated in February 1943, the long island-hopping road to Tokyo had begun.

waypoints.title: Uncommon valor at Iwo Jima
waypoints.label: The last approaches
waypoints.narrative: By 1945 the island-hopping campaign reached Japan's doorstep. Iwo Jima — eight square miles of volcanic rock, needed as a fighter base and emergency landing field for B-29s — was defended from a honeycomb of tunnels by 21,000 men ordered to die in place; nearly all did. The Marines took 26,000 casualties in five weeks, the only major battle where American casualties exceeded Japanese. Joe Rosenthal's photograph of the flag-raising on Mount Suribachi, taken on the battle's fifth day, became the most reproduced image of the war.

image.caption: Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima, Mount Suribachi, 23 February 1945 (Joe Rosenthal)

resources.label: Wikipedia: Raising the Flag on Iwo Jima

waypoints.title: Surrender in Tokyo Bay
waypoints.label: The war ends
waypoints.narrative: After the firebombing of Japan's cities, the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, and the Soviet declaration of war, Emperor Hirohito broadcast Japan's surrender on 15 August 1945 — the first time most Japanese had ever heard his voice. On 2 September, aboard the battleship USS Missouri in Tokyo Bay, the instrument of surrender was signed in twenty-three minutes while hundreds of Allied carrier aircraft roared overhead. General MacArthur closed the ceremony: 'These proceedings are closed.' The Second World War — six years and some sixty million lives — was over.

image.caption: The Japanese surrender delegation aboard USS Missouri, 2 September 1945
